data.title: South Plains Financial (SPFI) Margin Surge Reinforces Bullish Valuation Narrative

With current earnings growth pacing well ahead of its five-year average and forecasts calling for earnings and revenue growth of just 4.2% and 6.3% per year respectively, investors may see the company’s strong profit metrics offset by expectations for slower gains going forward. The stock trades at a price-to-earnings ratio of 10.5x, below both industry and peer averages and well under its estimated fair value, suggesting room for upside as margins and growth remain robust.

data.title: A Fresh Look at CN Rail After 4% Price Rebound and Regulatory Headlines
data.description:
Thinking about Canadian National Railway’s stock right now? You’re not alone. Whether you’re holding onto shares, considering getting in, or mulling over your next move, there’s real value in stepping back and weighing where this iconic railroad stands. With markets buzzing and economic opinions shifting week to week, Canadian National’s stock hasn’t just coasted along the rails; it has had a journey of its own.

After a rough start to the year, the stock price has rebounded recently, rising 4.2% over the past month and trimming earlier losses. Still, looking further back, the share price sits about 11.3% lower than it did a year ago. What’s behind these moves? Industry chatter and headlines have pointed to ongoing adjustments in North American freight volumes and supply chain recovery. There has also been talk about potential regulatory tweaks that could reshape the competitive landscape down the line. While none of these headlines have been seismic, together they have kept investors watching both opportunity and risk on the horizon.

So what about value? A quick glance at our valuation checks gives Canadian National Railway a score of 3 out of 6 for being undervalued—a mixed but intriguing report card. Where does that leave you if you’re thinking like a valuation-first investor? Let’s dig into the different methods for evaluating a blue-chip industrial like this, then explore a smarter way to tie it all together at the end.

Why Canadian National Railway is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: Canadian National Railway Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s intrinsic value by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. In Canadian National Railway’s case, this means looking at how much cash the business could generate in the coming years, adjusting for risk and the time value of money.

Currently, Canadian National Railway generates CA$3.45 Billion in free cash flow. Analyst forecasts expect a steady rise, with free cash flow projected to reach CA$4.26 Billion by 2029. After the first five years, Simply Wall St extrapolates the rest using historical and industry trends, suggesting ongoing modest growth out past 2030. All these cash flows are estimated in Canadian dollars.

The DCF model here uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. After tallying up the discounted future cash flows, the intrinsic value comes out to CA$124.85 per share. When compared with today’s share price, this represents a 6.9% premium and suggests the stock is slightly overvalued but close to fair value.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Canadian National Railway.

Simply Wall St performs a valuation analysis on every stock in the world every day (check out Canadian National Railway's valuation analysis). We show the entire calculation in full. You can track the result in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted when this changes.

Approach 2: Canadian National Railway Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Canadian National Railway, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a popular way to assess whether the stock represents good value. The PE ratio measures what investors are willing to pay today for a dollar of current earnings, making it particularly useful for established firms with steady profit streams.

Determining what a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio should be is not always straightforward. It heavily depends on earnings growth expectations and the risks involved. Higher expected earnings growth or lower business risk can justify paying up for a higher PE multiple. In contrast, slower growth or unique risks may pull it lower.

data.title: Gentex (GNTX) Margin Dip Reinforces Value Narrative Versus Peers This Earnings Season
data.description:
Gentex (GNTX) closed the period with net profit margins of 16.6%, showing a slight pullback from last year’s 17.6%. While five-year earnings have grown at a steady 3.7% annual pace, management now forecasts earnings to accelerate by 12.3% per year and revenue by 6.4% per year. Both figures are just below US market averages. With high earnings quality, an attractive dividend, and no significant flagged risks, Gentex’s discounted valuation versus peers may help drive investor interest through earnings season.

Margin Expansion Forecasts Gain Credibility

  • Analysts expect Gentex’s profit margins to climb from 16.6% today to 17.9% in three years, suggesting improved operational leverage and product mix as the company integrates new technologies and cost savings programs.
  • The analysts' consensus view highlights that Gentex’s ongoing initiatives, such as manufacturing automation, a focus on high-margin products, and targets set after the VOXX acquisition, are seen as catalysts for further margin gains over the next 24 months.
    • Consensus notes these improvements reinforce a constructive case for sustained profitability even as the broader US auto components industry faces rising input costs.
    • Analysts see the company’s margin progress distinguishing it from peers who are struggling with margin pressure in a competitive sector.

    • Analysts highlight that even with automotive sector headwinds, these expansion strategies position Gentex for more consistent revenue streams beyond traditional mirrors.
